source_label: Wiktionary — さかな (sakana): Tokyo pitch accent [sàkáná] heiban [0], IPA [sa̠ka̠na̠], "fish"
notes: Sakana AI is the Tokyo research lab behind the AI Scientist and evolutionary model merging, co-founded by David Ha and Llion Jones; the name is the Japanese word for fish (魚 / さかな), evoking a school of fish forming a coherent whole from simple rules. Japanese says it flat and even-weighted — sah kah nah, three even morae (heiban, no accented syllable). English speakers commonly anglicize it to suh-KAH-nuh; the reading to avoid is the banana-rhyming suh-KAN-uh, which flattens the middle vowel to the wrong quality.

source_label: Wikipedia — Surya (/ˈsuːriə/ SOO-ree-ə), the Hindu sun god the project is named after; Wiktionary Sanskrit सूर्य [ˈsuːɾjɐ]
notes: Surya is Datalab's open-source OCR, layout and table-recognition model and Python package (surya-ocr), the engine behind Marker. The README says it is named for the Hindu sun god, Sanskrit/Hindi sources support SOOR-yuh (two syllables, long oo, y-glide), while English Wikipedia gives the anglicized three-syllable SOO-ree-uh; both are attested and no source establishes a winner. The common mistake is reading the first vowel like surrey.

source_label: Wiktionary — सर्व (Sanskrit sarva-, IPA /s̪ɐɾ.ʋɐ/, stress on the first syllable; sarvam = "all, everything")
notes: Sarvam AI is India's IndiaAI-Mission sovereign-model builder; its open Sarvam-M, Sarvam-30B and Sarvam-105B models ship on Hugging Face and are the default reference point for Indic-language LLM work. The name is Sanskrit सर्वम् "all, everything", where the inherent short a is a central /ə~ɐ/ — so it is SUR-vum, stressed on the first syllable, not "SAR-vam" with the vowel of "car".

source_label: Wiktionary — 快手 (pinyin kuàishǒu, Sinological IPA /kʰu̯aɪ̯⁵¹ ʂoʊ̯²¹⁴/)
notes: Kuaishou (快手, pinyin kuàishǒu, "quick hand") is the Chinese short-video company behind the Kling video model and the Kwai-Keye / KwaiVGI open models on Hugging Face. "Kuai" is one syllable that rhymes with "why" — which is exactly why the company's overseas brand is spelled Kwai — and "shou" rhymes with "show". So it is KWAI-show, not "koo-eye-shoo" or "KWAY-shoo".

source_label: Jina AI official FAQ — "How do I pronounce Jina?": "like jee-na with the emphasis on the jee. Or exactly the way the name 'Gina' is pronounced in English."
notes: Jina AI is the Berlin-based search-AI company (acquired by Elastic in October 2025) behind the jina-embeddings and jina-reranker model families and the r.jina.ai reader endpoint used in RAG and agent stacks. Its official FAQ answers "How do I pronounce Jina?" with: roughly like jee-na, stress on the jee — exactly the way the English name "Gina" is said. The same FAQ explicitly calls out jy-na and jee-NA (stress on the second syllable) as incorrect, so the Spanish-style HEE-nah and German-style YEE-nah readings the spelling suggests are wrong too.

source_label: GitHub — vltpkg org display name: "vlt /vōlt/" (mirrored on vlt.io and docs.vlt.sh page titles)
notes: vlt is a JavaScript package manager and CLI from npm's creator Isaac Schlueter and former npm maintainers, released with vsr, a serverless registry. Because the name is written with no vowels, cold readers spell it out as "V-L-T" or guess "vult", and many write-ups call it "vault" — but the project brands itself "vlt /vōlt/" in its own GitHub org name and site titles, so it is one syllable, exactly like the English word volt, rhyming with bolt.

source_label: Merriam-Webster — cogito (ˈkä-gi-ˌtō, ˈkō-gi-ˌtō, ˈkä-ji-ˌtō)
notes: Cogito is Deep Cogito's family of open-weight reasoning models (Cogito v2, and the v2.1 671B MoE on Hugging Face), named after Descartes' Latin cogito ergo sum. The stress lands on the first syllable in English — never "koh-JEE-toh" — but the g is unsettled: Merriam-Webster and Collins both lead with the hard-g KAH-gi-toh, while the anglicized soft-g KAH-ji-toh (by analogy with cogitate) and the Latinate KOH-ghee-toh are also attested. Deep Cogito has never stated a preference, and note this is a different word from the existing cognito entry (AWS Cognito, kog-NEE-toh).

source_label: GitHub — typst/typst README ("IPA: /taɪpst/. 'Ty' like in Typesetting and 'pst' like in Hipster.")
notes: Typst is a Rust-based markup typesetting system pitched as a modern LaTeX replacement, driven from the command line with typst compile. Its official README settles the pronunciation with IPA /taɪpst/: one single syllable, "Ty" as in Typesetting plus "pst" as in Hipster. It is NOT "typist" — there is no vowel between the p and the st.

source_label: Zenn — Trying Out Qoder (pronunciation given as /ˈkoʊdər/, "similar to coder")
notes: Alibaba's agentic AI IDE (Quest Mode, spec-driven agents), launched Aug 2025 with 1.0 in May 2026. Pronounced exactly like "coder" — the Q is a straight substitution for the C and adds no sound of its own. Chinese launch coverage describes the name as 谐音 Coder (a homophone of "Coder"); the common misreadings are the letter-name "Q-oder" and "KWOH-der".

source_label: Anchore OSS Docs — "Grype (rhymes with hype) is an open-source vulnerability scanner"
notes: Grype is Anchore's open-source vulnerability scanner for container images, filesystems and SBOMs, the companion to Syft. Anchore's official docs spell it out: "Grype (rhymes with hype)" — so it is simply the word "gripe". The y-spelling misleads readers into "grip", "GRIP-ee" or a French-looking "gree-PAY", all of which are wrong.

source_label: Anchore OSS Docs — Syft (pronounced like "sift")
notes: Anchore's open-source CLI that generates a Software Bill of Materials from container images and filesystems, and the SBOM feeder for its vulnerability scanner Grype. Anchore's own docs gloss it "pronounced like sift" — one syllable, exactly like the verb. The y trips people up because sibling tool Grype takes the other y sound and rhymes with hype, so readers guess "SYE-ft".

source_label: Wikipedia — Sandworm (Dune): name derived from Arabic shay' khulud, "thing of immortality"
notes: Shai-Hulud is the self-replicating npm supply-chain worm that hit thousands of packages from September 2025 through the "Shai-Hulud 2.0" and "Mini Shai-Hulud" waves, drawing a CISA alert and Microsoft/Unit 42 advisories; it is named for the Dune sandworm. Say it "shy-hoo-LOOD" with stress on the last syllable, from Arabic shay' khulud ("thing of immortality") — the reading used in the Dune films and in Frank Herbert's own recorded readings. Cold readers reach for "SHAY hyoo-lud" or "shy-HULL-ud"; a reduced middle vowel ("shy-huh-LOOD") is a valid variant.

source_label: NSA Ghidra FAQ — "Gee-druh. The G sounds like the G in goto, great, good, graph, and GitHub. The emphasis goes on the first syllable."
notes: Ghidra is the NSA's open-source software reverse-engineering framework, widely used for binary analysis and now a common target for AI agent MCP servers. The 'gh' spelling tempts readers into a soft-G 'JEE-dra', but the official FAQ specifies a hard G as in GitHub with stress on the first syllable: GEE-druh. Wikipedia gives the same IPA, and the question was settled in the project's own GitHub issue #61.

alt_respelling_us: oh diss yoos|uh diss ee us
source_label: Wiktionary — Odysseus (English IPA: /əˈdɪsjuːs/, /oʊˈdɪsi.əs/, /oʊˈdɪsjuːs/); Wikipedia gives /əˈdɪsiəs/
notes: Odysseus is PewDiePie's MIT-licensed self-hosted AI workspace (chat, agents, deep research, local models via Ollama/llama.cpp/vLLM), which went from zero to roughly 30k GitHub stars in 48 hours and past 77k within three weeks. The name is the Greek hero of the Odyssey and the stress lands on the second syllable: oh-DISS-ee-us, not oh-dee-SAY-us or o-DIE-see-us. The compressed variant oh-DISS-yoos is also standard English, and an unstressed first syllable (uh-DISS-ee-us) is the form Wikipedia lists.

source_label: Wiktionary — melange: /məˈlɑnʒ/, /meɪ-/, /-ˈlɑndʒ/ (corroborated by Dictionary.com: [mey-lahnzh, -lahnj])
notes: melange is Chainguard's declarative apk build tool; paired with apko it builds Wolfi-based low-CVE container images without a Dockerfile. The name is the French word mélange ("mixture"), so the final -ange is a soft "zh" like rouge or measure, and the stress falls on the second syllable: may-LAHNZH, not "MEL-anj". The anglicized may-LAHNJ is a listed dictionary variant, but the zh-form is the standard one.

source_label: Red Hat — What are SPIFFE and SPIRE? (SPIFFE, pronounced "spiffy")
notes: SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework For Everyone) is a CNCF graduated standard that gives every workload a cryptographic identity (an SVID) so services can do zero-trust mTLS without baked-in secrets; its implementation is SPIRE. It is spoken as a word, not letter-by-letter, and the trailing -E is voiced: it is pronounced exactly like the English word "spiffy", rhyming with jiffy. The two traps are saying "spiff" with a silent E, or spelling it out S-P-I-F-F-E.

source_label: youki README — About the name ("youki is pronounced as /joʊki/ or yoh-key")
notes: Container runtime implementing the OCI runtime-spec in Rust, a runc alternative. The official README and docs state it is pronounced /joʊki/ or "yoh-key", after the Japanese word 容器 (yōki) meaning "a container". The "ou" is a Japanese long o, so it is not "YOO-kee".

source_label: GitHub — krkn-chaos/krkn README ("Krkn aka Kraken")
notes: Krkn is a CNCF sandbox chaos and resiliency testing tool for Kubernetes, originally built at Red Hat as "Kraken". The vowel-less spelling is pure branding: it is simply said "kraken", which the maintainers confirm in the README title "Krkn aka Kraken" and in the code (the entry point is still run_kraken.py). Dictionaries give KRAH-ken as the standard reading of kraken, with KRAY-ken and KRACK-en as common variants.

source_label: GitHub README — aeraki-mesh/aeraki ("Aeraki [Air-rah-ki] is the Greek word for 'breeze'")
notes: Aeraki Mesh is a CNCF sandbox project that manages non-HTTP layer-7 protocols such as Dubbo, Thrift and Redis in an Istio service mesh. The maintainers spell the pronunciation out in the README: Aeraki [Air-rah-ki] is the Greek word for breeze, with the stress on the second syllable. The ae- digraph misleads English readers into air-uh-kye or ay-RAY-kee.

source_label: Kured official FAQ — "How do you pronounce kured?" → "cure dee" (IPA: /kjʊəɹdiː/)
notes: Kured (KUbernetes REboot Daemon) is a CNCF Sandbox DaemonSet that performs safe automatic node reboots, widely used on AKS and self-managed clusters. Its official FAQ answers the pronunciation question directly: say it as "cure dee", because the trailing D comes from "daemon", akin to systemd. The obvious cold reading — one syllable rhyming with "cured" — is the common mistake; the D is spoken separately, exactly as in containerd and etcd.

source_label: GitHub — apache/fluss README ("Fluss (German: river, pronounced /flus/)")
notes: Apache Fluss (incubating) is a Flink-native streaming storage layer for real-time lakehouses, tiering data into Iceberg or Paimon. The name is the German word for "river" and the project README states it is pronounced /flus/ — a short German u, closest to English "floos" — so it is not English "fuss", and the double-s stays voiceless rather than turning into a z.

source_label: Eclipse Zenoh README — "Zenoh (pronounced /zeno/)"
notes: Eclipse Zenoh is a zero-overhead pub/sub, store/query and compute protocol, now an official ROS 2 middleware via rmw_zenoh. Its README annotates it "(pronounced /zeno/)" — the trailing h is silent and it is just two syllables, never "ZEE-noh-h" or "zeh-NOH". English speakers usually say it like the philosopher Zeno, while the maintainers' own /zeno/ notation and many European users give ZEH-noh.

source_label: CNCF Sandbox application #56 — maintainers' project summary: "Kuasar(Quasar in Kubernetes) is a low-level container runtime"
notes: Kuasar is a CNCF Sandbox multi-sandbox container runtime written in Rust (Huawei Cloud, WasmEdge, openEuler) that unifies MicroVM, Wasm and app-kernel sandboxes behind one sandbox API. The name is simply "quasar" respelled with a K for Kubernetes — the maintainers' own CNCF application glosses it as "Kuasar(Quasar in Kubernetes)" — so it is said KWAY-zar, exactly like the astronomical object. Cold readers routinely mangle it as "koo-AH-sar" by reading the ua literally.

source_label: Dictionary.com — kairos: "kahy-rahs, -rohs" /ˈkaɪ rɑs, -roʊs/ (corroborated by Wiktionary /ˈkaɪɹɒs/)
notes: Kairos is a CNCF Sandbox project: an immutable Linux meta-distribution for building bootable edge Kubernetes OS images, used alongside Talos and Flatcar in edge fleets. The name is Ancient Greek καιρός, "the opportune moment", and the 'ai' digraph is /aɪ/ with stress on the first syllable: KYE-ross. Readers coming to it cold often say "KAY-ross" or "kah-ee-ROSS", both of which are wrong; a valid variant ends in -rohs.

source_label: Wikipedia — Piraeus (IPA /paɪˈriːəs, pɪˈreɪəs/)
notes: Piraeus Datastore is a CNCF sandbox project providing LINSTOR/DRBD-based replicated block storage for Kubernetes, a common Longhorn/OpenEBS alternative on bare-metal clusters; it is named after Greece's great port, Πειραιάς. The dominant English reading is "py-REE-us" — the -aeus ending is a trap that invites "PIE-ray-oos" or stress on the first syllable. Note that "pih-RAY-us" is not an error but a documented secondary variant: Wikipedia, Collins and Dictionary.com all list both /paɪˈriːəs/ and /pɪˈreɪəs/.

source_label: Oxford Learner's Dictionaries — vineyard (NAmE /ˈvɪnjərd/)
notes: Vineyard (v6d) is a CNCF sandbox project — an in-memory immutable data manager giving zero-copy sharing of distributed data between steps of big-data and ML pipelines. Despite the spelling it is VIN-yerd, with a short i and a silent e; it is not VINE-yard. Dictionaries are unanimous on /ˈvɪnjərd/, and the project site offers no alternative pronunciation of its own.

source_label: Dictionary.com — gravitino /ˌgræv ɪˈti noʊ/ (grav-i-TEE-noh)
notes: Apache Gravitino is a federated metadata lake / open data catalog (originally from Datastrato) that competes with Unity Catalog and Polaris for Iceberg governance. The name is the physics particle — graviton plus the Italian diminutive -ino — and dictionaries give grav-i-TEE-noh, stress on 'tee', with 'vi' as a short 'ih' rather than 'vye'. The project publishes no pronunciation guidance of its own, so the dictionary reading of the particle is the consensus.

source_label: jOOQ official — How to Pronounce jOOQ (IPA "dʒuːk", "Sounds like: juke")
notes: jOOQ is a typesafe SQL DSL for Java that models SQL as an internal DSL, widely used as an alternative to JPA/Hibernate. It is a recursive acronym for "jOOQ Object Oriented Querying", but it is never spelled out letter-by-letter — the maintainers keep a dedicated page stating it is pronounced /dʒuːk/, exactly like the "juke" in jukebox. The official blog compares it to SQL: just as you say "sequel" rather than "ess queue ell", you say "juke" rather than "jay o o queue".

source_label: Rocq wiki — Alternative names ("in English, sound like rock"; short for Rocquencourt)
notes: The Rocq Prover is the new name of the Coq proof assistant, official since the 9.0 release in March 2025. It is pronounced simply "rock" — the name honors Inria Rocquencourt, where the system was born, and echoes the mythical bird Roc; the final q is silent. Do not carry over the old Coq /kɒk/ vowel or read the French -cq as "kew".

source_label: Ktor FAQ (JetBrains) — "How do I pronounce Ktor?" → /keɪ-tor/
notes: Ktor is JetBrains' Kotlin-first framework for building asynchronous servers and clients, and the default HTTP stack for Kotlin backends and Kotlin Multiplatform. The official FAQ says it is pronounced /keɪ-tor/ — the name is the abbreviation ctor (constructor) with the C replaced by K for Kotlin, so the leading K is said as the letter name. Readers who see the vowel-less Kt spelling often guess kuh-TOR or spell it out K-T-O-R; both are wrong.

source_label: HikariCP README — "Hi·ka·ri [hi·ka·'lē] (Origin: Japanese): light; ray."
notes: Hikari is the Japanese word 光 ("light; ray") and the name behind HikariCP, the high-performance JDBC connection pool that Spring Boot uses by default — hence the ubiquitous "HikariPool-1 - Starting..." log line. The README's own heading spells the pronunciation out as Hi·ka·ri [hi·ka·'lē]: hee-kah-REE, three even syllables with the Japanese flapped r sounding close to an English l. English readers commonly get it wrong as "HIGH-kah-ree" with a long English i; the trailing CP (Connection Pool) is then spelled out as C-P.

source_label: Wiktionary — Werkzeug (German) /ˈvɛʁkˌtsɔʏ̯k/
notes: The WSGI library under Flask, German for "tool" — Pallets' own project page says so. German spelling trips up English readers four ways: W is a V, z is "ts", eu is "oy", and the final g devoices to a k, giving "VERK-tsoyk". The common English readings "WERK-zoog" and "WORK-zeeg" are simply wrong.

source_label: Jinja FAQ — "Jinja is a Japanese Shinto shrine (神社)"
notes: Jinja is the Python template engine behind Flask, Ansible and dbt, and the format Hugging Face chat templates are written in. The official FAQ says the name is the Japanese word 神社 (jinja, a Shinto shrine), chosen because temple puns on template — so both j's are soft: JIN-juh. Non-native readers who give j its native value and say "YIN-ya" or "HIN-ha" are the common miss.

source_label: Merriam-Webster — zephyr (ˈze-fər)
notes: Zephyr is the Linux Foundation's small-footprint RTOS for microcontrollers, and the default runtime behind Nordic's nRF Connect SDK plus growing Espressif and ST support. It is pronounced ZEF-ur (/ˈzɛfər/) — stress on the first syllable, a short 'e', the 'ph' as /f/, and the final 'y' reduced to a schwa — after Zephyrus, the Greek god of the west wind. Non-native devs routinely misread the Greek-via-Latin spelling as ZEE-fire, zeh-FEER or ZEP-hire.

source_label: GitHub — fxn/zeitwerk README §Pronunciation (audio recorded "in perfect German")
notes: Zeitwerk is the Ruby code loader that has been Rails' default autoloader since Rails 6 and remains so in Rails 8 The name is German for "clockwork" and follows German spelling rules — Z is "ts", "ei" is "eye", W is "v" — giving TSITE-verk, not the common misreading "ZEET-werk".

source_label: Citus Data blog — "How do you pronounce Citus?" (pronounced "site-us")
notes: Citus is the open-source extension that turns PostgreSQL into a distributed, sharded database, and it powers Azure Cosmos DB for PostgreSQL. It is pronounced SITE-us, not KEE-tus or SIT-us — the name comes from the Olympic motto Citius, Altius, Fortius (faster, higher, stronger). Citus Data got asked so often that they published an official blog post settling it.

source_label: GitHub — apache/apisix issue #1190, maintainer @membphis: "'6' is a lucky number in China, so we would like to call it `API SIX`"
notes: Apache APISIX is a top-level Apache Software Foundation cloud-native API gateway and AI gateway, a peer of Kong, Traefik and Envoy. Despite looking like one word, it is spoken as two tokens: the letters A-P-I followed by the number six. Maintainer @membphis confirmed this on the project tracker, explaining that 6 is a lucky number in China — hence the name.

source_label: Merriam-Webster — alembic (ə-ˈlem-bik)
notes: Alembic is the schema-migration tool for SQLAlchemy, used in most FastAPI and SQLModel backends. The name comes from the alchemical still, borrowed from Arabic al-anbiq via Greek ambix. Stress falls on the middle syllable — uh-LEM-bik — not on the first, which is what the spelling tempts most non-native speakers into.

source_label: seanmonstar — "Introducing Reqwest" (name origin: the `request` crate name was already taken)
notes: reqwest is the de-facto HTTP client crate of the Rust ecosystem, written by the author of hyper. The name is a deliberate misspelling of "request": the creator's introduction post explains it was forced on him because the `request` crate name was already taken, and it is said exactly like the ordinary English word "request". Cold readers often guess "REK-west" because the qw digraph is almost nonexistent in English outside "qwerty".

source_label: Wikipedia — Cython (/ˈsaɪθɒn/)
notes: Optimizing compiler that turns Python-like .pyx code into C extension modules; it powers the fast paths in NumPy, SciPy, pandas and scikit-learn. Pronounced "SIGH-thon" — the C is soft, rhyming with Python, not "see-thon" or "kye-thon". Do not confuse it with CPython, the reference interpreter, which really is said "see-Python".

source_label: Leiningen official FAQ — "How do you pronounce Leiningen? It's LINE-ing-en. ['laɪnɪŋən]"
notes: Leiningen (the lein command) is the long-standing build tool and project-automation entry point for Clojure. The official FAQ settles it: LINE-ing-en, with the German ei read as "eye" — the name comes from Carl Stephenson's short story "Leiningen Versus the Ants". Common misreadings like "lay-nin-jen" or "lee-nin-gen" are simply wrong.

source_label: FuriosaAI — RNGD product page ("RNGD (pronounced \"renegade\")")
notes: RNGD is FuriosaAI's second-generation data-center inference NPU (48GB HBM3, 180W, tensor-contraction architecture), pitched as a power-efficient Nvidia alternative and in mass production since January 2026. The vowel-less name is a disemvoweled spelling of "renegade" — Furiosa's own product page and its Hot Chips 2024 press release both gloss it as RNGD (pronounced "renegade"), and the company brands its spec page and user summit as RENEGADE. Reading it letter-by-letter as R-N-G-D or guessing "ringed" is the common mistake.

source_label: sierra-research/tau2-bench README — "How do you say τ³-bench? We just say 'tau three,' but you do you!"
notes: Sierra's τ-bench (now τ²/τ³-bench) is the tool-agent-user benchmark whose pass^k scores are cited in most 2025-2026 model cards. The leading character is the Greek letter tau, and the maintainers' README answers "How do you say τ³-bench?" with "We just say 'tau three'" — so it is the letter name, never "TAO" as in Daoism. In General American tau is /taʊ/, rhyming with "now"; the /tɔː/ ("taw") variant is also widely heard.

source_label: Aisera — A2A protocol explainer: AGNTCY (pronounced "agency")
notes: AGNTCY is the Linux Foundation-hosted "Internet of Agents" project donated by Cisco (Outshift), covering agent discovery (OASF), messaging (SLIM) and identity, and is routinely compared against MCP and A2A. It is a disemvoweled brand — AGNT (agent) plus -CY — and is simply read as the English word "agency" (AY-jun-see), not spelled out letter-by-letter. Cold readers usually try "A-G-N-T-C-Y" or "ag-EN-tik-ee"; the project's own site and the Linux Foundation press release never state the pronunciation, so this is labeled community-consensus on the strength of third-party technical coverage.

source_label: Wiktionary — Gaia (Gaia hypothesis: /ˈɡaɪə/; goddess: /ˈɡeɪə/, /ˈɡaɪə/); Wikipedia — Gaia (/ˈɡeɪ.ə, ˈɡaɪ.ə/)
notes: GAIA (General AI Assistants) is the 466-question benchmark for browsing and tool-use agents, with a live Hugging Face leaderboard and its own unit in the HF Agents Course. The name reuses the Greek earth goddess, which English reads two ways: dictionaries list both /ˈɡaɪə/ (guy-uh) and /ˈɡeɪə/ (gay-uh). The authors never stated an official pronunciation, but scientific naming after the goddess — Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis — is settled on guy-uh, so that is the safer default.

source_label: Oxford Learner's Dictionaries — sycophancy /ˈsɪkəfənsi/ (NAmE & BrE); Dictionary.com lists /ˈsɪk ə fən si, -ˌfæn-, ˈsaɪ kə-/
notes: In LLM work, sycophancy is the failure mode where a model excessively agrees with or flatters the user, and it is now a standard eval axis reported in system cards and release notes. Stress falls on the FIRST syllable — SICK-uh-fuhn-see — and the opening "sy" is /sɪ/ like "sick", not "sigh". The tempting si-KOF-uhn-see reading, formed by analogy with discrepancy or despondency, is not recorded in any major dictionary; note also that the adjective sycophantic does shift the stress, to sik-uh-FAN-tik.

source_label: Wikipedia — Elo rating system (footnote: written as "Elo", not "ELO"; /ˈiːloʊ/ or /ˈɛloʊ/)
notes: The rating scale behind LMArena / Chatbot Arena leaderboards, cited as "Elo score" in most 2025-2026 model launch posts and eval blogs. It is the surname of Hungarian-American physics professor Arpad Elo (né Élő), not an acronym — Wikipedia states it is "written as 'Elo', not 'ELO'" — so spelling it out as letters E-L-O is the common mistake. Both EE-loh and EL-oh are accepted in English, with EE-loh listed first by Wikipedia and Wiktionary.

source_label: Wiktionary — liger, GenAm /ˈlaɪɡɚ/, blend of lion + tiger
notes: LinkedIn's Liger-Kernel is a Triton kernel library for LLM training, giving roughly 20% more throughput and 60% less memory, and it is wired into TRL, Axolotl, LLaMA-Factory and the Hugging Face Trainer. The name is the lion-tiger hybrid, so it is "LY-ger" with a long i and a hard g — not "LEE-ger", not short-i "LIG-er", and not soft-g "LY-jer". LinkedIn also backronyms it as GPU Efficient Runtime, but it is always spoken as the animal word.

source_label: Wikipedia — Helion (chemistry): "The term helion is a portmanteau of helium and ion"
notes: Helion is PyTorch's high-level kernel-authoring DSL from Meta (public beta at PyTorch Conference 2025), which compiles down to Triton. The name is the physics term for a helium-3 nucleus, a deliberate sibling to Triton (the hydrogen-3 nucleus), and it is a portmanteau of helium + ion — so the first syllable is HEE as in helium, not HELL as in the English word hellion. The reduced form hee lee uhn is an equally valid variant.

source_label: Wiktionary — chute: US /ʃut/, from French chute (Old French cheüe, Vulgar Latin cadūta)
notes: Chutes is Rayon Labs' serverless AI inference platform on Bittensor Subnet 64, and one of the highest-throughput providers routable from OpenRouter. The name is the ordinary English word chute (as in parachute or a garbage chute), borrowed from French, so the ch is /ʃ/ and it sounds exactly like shoots. Non-native developers reading it cold in a provider list routinely say chootz with an English ch, which is wrong.

source_label: Wiktionary — Romanian given name "Ion", IPA /iˈon/ (equivalent to English "John")
notes: Romanian-American Berkeley professor: co-creator of Apache Spark, Mesos and Ray, co-founder of Databricks and Anyscale, and head of the Sky Computing Lab behind vLLM and Chatbot Arena. "Ion" is not the chemistry word — it is the Romanian form of "John", /iˈon/, so "ee-OHN" and not "EYE-on". The surname is "STOY-kuh": the "oi" is a single diphthong (the name goes back to Bulgarian Stoyko), not "sto-EE-ka" or "STOH-ik-uh".

source_label: PronounceNames — Ghodsi (Iranian origin, "godsee"); cf. Wikipedia "Ghodsi" (Persian قدسی, from Ghods/Quds)
notes: Ali Ghodsi is the co-founder and CEO of Databricks, quoted in most lakehouse and AI-infrastructure news cycles. The surname is Persian (قدسی, from Ghods/Quds, "sacred"), where the "gh" transliterates a single hard G rather than an aspirated or two-step "guh-HOD". It is simply "GOD-see", two syllables with the stress up front.

source_label: Wikipedia — Liang Wenfeng (Chinese: 梁文锋; pinyin: Liáng Wénfēng)
notes: Founder and CEO of DeepSeek and co-founder of the High-Flyer hedge fund; the R1/V3 line reset open-weight model economics. Pinyin is Liáng Wénfēng: "Liang" is a single syllable, lyahng — not "lee-ANG" — and "Wenfeng" is wun-fung, with Wiktionary giving /li̯ɑŋ³⁵/, /wən³⁵/ and /fɤŋ⁵⁵/ for 梁文锋. The usual Western podcast reading "lee-ANG wen-FENG", with an English short-e in both halves, is simply wrong.

source_label: HowToPronounce — Raschka ("ra-shkuh", 25 user recordings)
notes: Sebastian Raschka, author of "Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)" and the Ahead of AI newsletter. German surname: "sch" is the /ʃ/ digraph, so it is "RAHSH-kuh", not a "rask" cluster. US speakers commonly anglicize the first vowel to "RASH-kuh"; both are heard.

source_label: VOA Pronunciation Guide — Vietnamese names (DAO TRONG LICH → "DOW …"; NGUYEN MINH TRIET → "… TREE-eht")
notes: Tri Dao is the author of FlashAttention and co-author of Mamba, and co-founder/Chief Scientist at Together AI; his name fronts the Dao-AILab kernels and Mamba model cards. The name is Vietnamese: "Tri" is TREE, not "try", and "Dao" (Đào) rhymes with "how" — it is neither the Taoist "tao" nor the crypto "DAO". VOA's Vietnamese pronunciation guide renders the surname Dao as "DOW" and the Tri- syllable as "TREE-".

source_label: Rustacean Station — host: "how do you pronounce your last name?"; Armin: "Ronacher."
notes: Armin Ronacher is the Austrian developer behind Flask and Jinja, now at Sentry, whose essays on agentic coding are HN front-page staples. The surname is German: the 'ch' follows a back vowel, so it is the ach-Laut (the throat sound in Scottish 'loch'), which English speakers render as a /k/ — ROH-nah-ker with stress on the first syllable, never the English 'ch' of 'church' and never a 'sh'. Anglophones routinely mangle it (the top user entry on howtopronounce.com is 'rah-nashur'), and on Rustacean Station the host had to stop and ask him how to say it.

source_label: Dictionary.com — Kolmogorov /ˌkɒlmɒˈɡɔːrɒf/ (stress on -GOR-, final -f)
notes: Andrey Kolmogorov, Russian mathematician behind Kolmogorov complexity and the theorem underpinning Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs). Stress lands on the third syllable — kohl-muh-GOR-uhf — and the final v is devoiced to an f, matching the Russian [kəlmɐˈɡorəf]. Cold readers usually get it wrong twice, stressing the first syllable and ending on a voiced ov.

source_label: Wiktionary — Huyền (Hà Nội [hwiən˨˩])
notes: Chip Huyen, author of O'Reilly's 'AI Engineering' and 'Designing Machine Learning Systems' (huyenchip.com). Vietnamese Huyền is a single syllable — Wiktionary gives Hanoi [hwiən˨˩], roughly 'hw' plus the name Ian; English readers wrongly stretch it into three syllables as 'HYOO-yen'. Commonly simplified in English to rhyme with 'when'.
